question.text: A trader executes a bullish vertical spread using puts on USDINR: sells an 83.00 put at Rs. 0.07 and buys an 82.50 put at Rs. 0.05. What is the BEP and maximum profit?

answer.text: BEP: Rs. 82.98, Maximum profit: Rs. 0.02 (when USDINR expires at or above 83.00)
BEP: Rs. 82.52, Maximum profit: Rs. 0.48 (when USDINR expires at or below 82.50)
BEP: Rs. 83.07, Maximum profit: Rs. 0.07 (when USDINR expires at or above 83.07)
BEP: Rs. 82.93, Maximum profit: Rs. 0.50 (when both options expire)

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Net premium received = 0.07 - 0.05 = Rs. 0.02 (max profit when both options expire worthless, i.e., USDINR ≥ 83.00). Max loss = Strike difference - Net premium = 0.50 - 0.02 = Rs. 0.48 (when USDINR ≤ 82.50). BEP = Higher strike - Net premium = 83.00 - 0.02 = Rs. 82.98. **The Trap:** This is a net credit strategy (receives premium). Maximum profit is the net premium, not the strike difference. BEP is below the higher strike. **Cross-Exam Context:** Series I Ch5 covers bull put spreads. Series XIII compares these across asset classes — mechanics are identical. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Bull put spread = credit spread with bullish view. You earn small premium if you're right, but risk larger loss if wrong. Always calculate max loss before entry.
